data.title: Learn From The Leaders: How To Research Stocks On Seeking Alpha | James Foord's Investing Process
data.description:
For more insights from Kirk Spano, join The Pragmatic Investor today!

Follow James Foord at The Pragmatic Investor on Seeking Alpha!

This video's transcript was generated by a third party. It is not curated or reviewed and is provided for convenience and information purposes only. The accuracy and completeness of the transcript are not guaranteed.

James Foord: Hi guys. This is James Foord from The Pragmatic Investor. And today, I'm going to take the chance to show you exactly how I use the Seeking Alpha platform to make my investment decisions. So, you're going to see everything that goes through my mind when I'm looking at different stocks. And basically, just what I do from the moment I wake up to the moment I go to sleep. So, let's jump right into it.

Alright. So, first off, this is basically, first thing I look at every morning is the Seeking Alpha main page. Hopefully, most days, you don't see all this red up here, but nonetheless, that's not important for right now. What I like to look at most of all is, this trending section. So, both the Trending Analysis and the Trending News. I think the Trending News is a great way to get a good sense of what's happening in the market. Usually you've got the most important news items, so that's certainly pretty good. Related to that you usually see some interesting articles on the trending section that probably related to some important news. Always great to stay on top of things just to look at this little trending section.

And, also, if you scroll a little bit further down, you also have another interesting trending section down here, which I also find quite useful. Here, you can see the most searched stocks on Seeking Alpha and also the most traded stocks. So, again, if something interesting is going on in markets, this is a great place to start, right?

We can also see here how these stocks have moved. For example, now we can see CrowdStrike. It's gone down 10%. Oh, what's happened? We could see ImmunityBio up 12%. Okay. So, we can then click into these tickers and have a look at what's happening. Right? Going to the main section, if there's anything important, you can find it here on the news. Oftentimes, especially with the popular stocks, there'll be some recent analysis that will probably shed some light, if there's been some big stock movements, but this is a good place to start your research, basically. I mean, CrowdStrike right now, as I understand, that's having some issues with the whole AI narrative, just the latest victim of the AI narrative, but what are you going to do?

Anyway, carrying on, when I'm looking at a stock to invest in, obviously, one of the first things I would do is, actually go into some of the different analysis. Now, what I found really helpful actually about Seeking Alpha is you get the different ratings here. So, I obviously like to get a balanced view of what's going on. So, I like to read an article that is maybe a Sell to see what the bulls are saying, what the bears are saying, and maybe a more neutral approach like someone who's got a Hold rating, right?

And our next stop, once you are doing research, what I find most useful potentially is, all this data we have here, valuation section, always really very useful. Growth, profitability, again, also interesting information. The earnings estimates is likely one of the most interesting because with the earnings estimates you can not only get a sense of the valuation, but you can get a sense of the forward valuation. Right? So, you can see here the Forward P/E throughout the years, upcoming years for CrowdStrike and also revenue estimates.

Another thing that I really like to do oftentimes, especially when I'm writing an article is compare a company to its peers, right? And that's very easy if we go into here into this peers section. This is definitely, again, very, very good because, obviously, stocks don't trade in a vacuum. They trade in a market. So, it's always very, very helpful to have a look at basically what stocks are doing, how they're for example, how the valuation relates to other stocks in the sector, things like this. Of course, I will say, sometimes you need to adapt the peers a little bit. I don't think necessarily that GlobalFoundries is a peer with CrowdStrike, so you have to pick your own when it comes to that. But obviously, very, very useful tool right there. Right?

And in terms of doing stock research, that's pretty much where I leave things. I mean, the other great thing about Seeking Alpha that I personally obviously use a lot is, any stock that you have in your portfolio or that you want to follow, you basically click here. You get all the updates straight to your inbox, which, again, is really, really useful.

And, yeah, and then there's the Factor Grades, of course. This is a nice little snapshot you can always have. That also includes the Quant Ratings, Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating, which can be quite handy at different times. And, yeah, that's pretty much what I do in terms of, on a preliminary basis having a quick look at what the company is doing. This, of course, whenever there's earnings, first thing you do, obviously, go to the Transcripts and Insights. Again, super, super valuable information.

I particularly like Seeking Alpha. It has the transcripts for the earnings calls. That's really, really good information because that's where you really get to see or hear rather management talk about the company, right? And, of course, got all the SEC filings here very nicely organized. Stuff that potentially you could find somewhere else. Well, SEC filings for sure, but just the way that it's organized on Seeking Alpha for me, it's probably the best, the best possible way you can organize it.

That's why, I mean, obviously, this is what I do, but Seeking Alpha is easily the website that I use the most. It's literally the first thing I look at when I wake up. So, yeah, that's about it when it comes to how I use Seeking Alpha.

The other thing you can do on Seeking Alpha also is, I guess, you can use the screener. That's pretty useful too. So, you can screen for different stocks that you like in different sectors. It's pretty, again, a pretty useful tool, but overall, that's just how I like to use Seeking Alpha, really keeping in the loop. And, again, once you do drill down on a stock that you like, you have all that great information on the actual stock page. And, again, more than anything, such great articles by all the amazing Seeking Alpha contributors and of course a great community, right? Just go into an article, and you can always find, especially on the more trending ones, great discussion. Like a lot of people, I sometimes spend more time on the comment section than I do on actual articles. But that's part of the beauty of Seeking Alpha. It's a platform, but it's also got that beautiful community element, right?

And that's why I love writing. That's why I love having The Pragmatic Investor, having my own little community, my own little space where I cover stocks from high-tech interesting growth stocks to also more secure stocks. We do a little bit of everything, right? So, a bit of technical analysis, fundamental, and big picture macro. So, anyway, that's it for this video. I hope you guys enjoyed this little breakdown. And, yeah, sign up to The Pragmatic Investor if you haven't yet.

For more insights from Kirk Spano, join The Pragmatic Investor today!

Follow James Foord at The Pragmatic Investor on Seeking Alpha!

data.title: Columbia Cornerstone Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

  • Columbia Cornerstone Growth Fund Institutional Class shares returned 2.19% for the three months ending December 31, 2025.
  • The fund's benchmark, the Russell 1000 Growth Index, returned 1.12% for the same period.
  • In sector terms, positioning within health care, consumer discretionary, financials and communication services led contributions to relative performance, while positioning within information technology and industrials weighed most heavily on performance.

Market overview

U.S. equities posted a gain of 2.41% in the fourth quarter, as measured by the Russell 1000 Index. The positive three-month return was primarily a function of the same factors that propelled stocks over the full year: namely, better-than-expected economic growth, robust corporate earnings results and inflation that largely remained below an annualized rate of 3%. The U.S. Federal Reserve continued to ease policy, cutting rates by a quarter point at its meetings in October and December and announcing an end to the multi-year effort to reduce the size of its balance sheet. Stocks also remained supported by ongoing excitement surrounding the artificial intelligence (AI) theme, albeit with a brief stretch of concern in November that AI-related equities were in a "bubble."

Notably, the quarter was characterized by a broadening of leadership away from the mega-cap technology stocks that had been key drivers of market performance for most of the year, as value stocks outpaced their growth counterparts. At the style level, value stocks rose 3.81% — based on the Russell 1000 Value Index — and outpaced the 1.12% gain for the Russell 1000 Growth Index.

Top holdings (% of net assets): as of December 31, 2025

NVIDIA 13.03
Apple 10.72
Microsoft 10.68
Amazon.com 6.17
Alphabet-A 5.37
Broadcom 4.79
Meta Platforms 4.41
Eli Lilly & Company 3.76
Visa 2.90
Netflix 2.11
Top holdings exclude short-term holdings and cash, if applicable. Fund holdings are as of the date given, are subject to change at any time, and are not recommendations to buy or sell any security.

Top five contributors - Effect on return (%) as of December 31, 2025

Alphabet-A 1.22
Eli Lilly & Company 1.01
Apple 0.69
Alphabet-C 0.58
Exact Sciences 0.43

Top five detractors - Effect on return (%) as of December 31, 2025

Microsoft -0.74
Oracle -0.57
Netflix -0.51
Meta Platforms -0.47
Uber Technologies -0.24

Quarterly portfolio recap

Within health care, positive contributions were led by Exact Sciences, as the provider of a home screening test for colon cancer was acquired by Abbott Laboratories at a significant premium to its trading price. Eli Lilly was another standout performer, posting strong earnings driven by its dominant position in obesity treatments, while providing favorable updates on an oral weight-loss drug in development.

Within consumer discretionary, travel website operator Expedia outperformed on strong earnings growth that exceeded expectations, driven by positive booking and profitability metrics, while raising guidance. The company is well positioned through its operational improvements to benefit from a strong travel backdrop. Amazon reported a strong quarter that handily beat expectations, supported by results in its cloud computing and AI hyperscaler businesses. In addition, advertising revenue from its online shopping platform was higher than expected.

Sector weights (%): fund vs. benchmark as of December 31, 2025

Within financials, the overweight exposure to large banks, including Citigroup, Goldman Sachs Group and Bank of America proved beneficial. Sentiment around these stocks benefited from strong earnings reports driven by increased capital markets activity and strong trading results. In addition, an improving regulatory outlook should support continued capital returns to shareholders.

Alphabet highlighted contributions within communication services. The provider of the Google search engine had been viewed as a potential “AI loser”, but has flipped that narrative based on the impressive performance of its Gemini generative AI assistant and share gains for its large language model used to generate predictive text. In addition, Alphabet reported strong advertising revenue from its Google search and YouTube streaming segments.

On the downside, within information technology, shares of ServiceNow declined in the quarter on news that the workplace productivity platform provider planned to acquire cybersecurity company Armis. The market appeared to react to the short-term impact of the deal on earnings and potential challenges around integration. We believe these concerns are overblown, and we view the acquisition as strategically sound with respect to ServiceNow's existing capabilities and growth opportunities. Shares of cybersecurity company Palo Alto Networks also moved lower on news of an agreement to acquire Chronosphere, a provider of "observability" software used to diagnose IT system issues. While the deal comes with some execution risk and near-term pressure on free cash flow, we believe the acquisition fits well with Palo Alto's strategy and opens up a new avenue of growth. Database software and enterprise application provider, Oracle was another laggard. The company posted results in keeping with expectations and reported a strong order backlog. However, investors have become concerned about the company's rising capital expenditures and increasing debt financing to support the company's significant backlog of cloud infrastructure contracts, as well as customer concentration risk, given its massive long-term deal to support OpenAI's data centers. We view the growth trajectory for Oracle as attractive, and we expect that improving margins will support higher free cash flow than anticipated and help ease concerns around financing.

Within industrials, shares of ride sharing and meal delivery company Uber technologies moved lower after the company reported strong bookings but lower-than-expected profitability, with management saying that it is deliberately moderating the pace of margin expansion in order to continue reinvesting in the business. In addition, reports that Waymo, which partners with Uber in some cities, was independently expanding its autonomous ride-hailing service in four new cities weighed on the stock. Power management company Eaton underperformed after providing guidance that was disappointing relative to high expectations. Eaton remains well positioned to benefit from the buildout of AI data centers, which require intense power use and advanced technology to dissipate heat. We expect the company to experience accelerated growth as it integrates recent acquisitions and recent capacity additions begin to contribute to revenue.

While selection was generally positive within communication services, social media company Meta Platform was a laggard, with shares pulling back on the announcement of plans to significantly increase spending on efforts to develop artificial general intelligence and integrate it across its platforms. Shares of Netflix declined as the streaming service reported an earnings miss primarily driven by a one-time tax-related charge. The stock then dipped again on news of a proposed acquisition of Warner Bros. Discovery raised questions about Netflix's strategy, given the company has historically preferred to invest in generating its own original content.

Outlook

We remain constructive on the outlook for equities, based on improving business confidence, a renewed Fed easing cycle and economic tailwinds to capital spending from the recently passed budget bill. Signs of a recovery in lending and capital markets activity further support the outlook. Risks around labor market softening and the impact of tariffs remain, but they appear manageable in the context of broader economic resilience, especially if inflation remains contained.

More broadly, we continue to focus on selecting stocks of companies with idiosyncratic drivers or secular growth tailwinds, with the potential to outperform in a wide range of economic environments. In so doing, we seek a combination of certain characteristics that we believe have the potential to outperform throughout a market cycle. Specifically, we emphasize high-quality established and emerging growth companies that share four characteristics: large and expanding market opportunities, sustainable and durable competitive advantages, improving free-cash-flow generation and accelerating returns on invested capital.

Average annual total returns (%) as of December 31, 2025

Columbia Cornerstone Growth Fund 3-mon. 1-year 3-year 5-year 10-year
Institutional Class 2.19 16.40 30.22 14.14 15.99
Class A without sales charge 2.12 16.10 29.89 13.86 15.69
Class A with 5.75% maximum sales charge -3.76 9.43 27.35 12.52 15.01
Russell 1000 Growth Index 1.12 18.56 31.15 15.32 18.13

Performance data shown represents past performance and is not a guarantee of future results. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data shown. Please visit Financial Professionals | Columbia Threadneedle Investments US for performance data current to the most recent month end. Institutional Class shares are sold at net asset value and have limited eligibility. Columbia Management Investment Distributors, Inc. offers multiple share classes, not all necessarily available through all firms, and the share class ratings may vary. Contact us for details.

Share ClassSymbol A (LEGAX) C (LEGCX) Institutional (GEGTX) Institutional 2 (CLWFX) Institutional 3 (CGFYX) R (CGWRX)

5-Year Morningstar Rating™

The Morningstar Rating is for the indicated share classes only as of 12/31/25; other classes may have different performance characteristics. The Morningstar ratings for the overall, three-, five- and ten-year periods for Class A shares are 3 stars, 3 stars, 4 stars and 3 stars and for Institutional Class shares are 3 stars, 3 stars, 4 stars and 3 stars among 1004, 1004, 936 and 755 Large Growth funds, respectively, and are based on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure.

Constructive on equities, supported by economic resilience, policy tailwinds and high-quality growth opportunities.

Fund strategy

Identifies unique views of what drives growth and takes a broad approach to growth investing

Assesses the risk of each underlying security to avoid unintended exposure

Expense ratio

Institutional 0.72% 0.72%

From the fund's most recent prospectus. Net expense ratio reflects a contractual fee waiver/expense reimbursement through 11/30/2026, unless sooner terminated at the sole discretion of the fund's board.

Columbia Cornerstone Growth (GEGTX) Fund

Effective September 1, 2025, the fund name changed from Columbia Large Cap Growth Fund to Columbia Cornerstone Growth Fund.

Investment Risks

Market risk may affect a single issuer, sector of the economy, industry or the market as a whole. Growth securities, at times, may not perform as well as value securities or the stock market in general and may be out of favor with investors. Foreign investments subject the fund to risks, including political, economic, market, social and others within a particular country, as well as to currency instabilities and less stringent financial and accounting standards generally applicable to U.S. issuers. The fund may invest significantly in issuers within a particular sector , which may be negatively affected by market, economic or other conditions, making the fund more vulnerable to unfavorable developments in the sector.

Investors should consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a mutual fund carefully before investing. For a free prospectus or a summary prospectus, which contains this and other important information about the funds, visit Financial Professionals | Columbia Threadneedle Investments US . Read the prospectus carefully before investing.

Columbia funds are distributed by Columbia Management Investment Distributors, Inc., member FINRA and managed by Columbia Management Investment Advisers, LLC.

Columbia Threadneedle Investments (Columbia Threadneedle) is the global brand name of the Columbia and Threadneedle group of companies.

The views expressed are as of the date given, may change as market or other conditions change and may differ from views expressed by other Columbia Management Investment Advisers, LLC (CMIA) associates or affiliates. Actual investments or investment decisions made by CMIA and its affiliates, whether for its own account or on behalf of clients, may not necessarily reflect the views expressed. This information is not intended to provide investment advice and does not take into consideration individual investor circumstances. Investment decisions should always be made based on an investor's specific financial needs, objectives, goals, time horizon and risk tolerance. Asset classes described may not be appropriate for all investors. Past performance does not guarantee future results, and no forecast should be considered a guarantee either. Since economic and market conditions change frequently, there can be no assurance that the trends described here will continue or that any forecasts are accurate.

Additional performance information: All results shown assume reinvestment of distributions and do not reflect the deduction of taxes that a shareholder would pay on fund distributions or the redemption of fund shares.

©2026 Morningstar. All rights Reserved. The Morningstar information contained herein: (1) is proprietary to Morningstar and/or its content providers; (2) may not be copied or distributed; and (3) is not warranted to be accurate, complete or timely. Neither Morningstar nor its content providers are responsible for any damages or losses arising from any use of this information. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

For each fund with at least a three-year history, Morningstar calculates a Morningstar Rating™ used to rank the fund against other funds in the same category. It is calculated based on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure that accounts for variation in a fund's monthly excess performance, without any adjustments for loads (front-end, deferred, or redemption fees), placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. Exchange-traded funds and open-ended mutual funds are considered a single population for comparative purposes. The top 10% of funds in each category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars and the bottom 10% receive 1 star (Each share class is counted as a fraction of one fund within this scale and rated separately, which may cause slight variations in the distribution percentages). The Overall Morningstar Rating for a fund is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its three-, five- and ten-year (if applicable) Morningstar Rating metrics.

Source: London Stock Exchange Group plc and its group undertakings (collectively, the "LSE Group"). © LSE Group 2026. FTSE Russell is a trading name of certain of the LSE Group companies. "FTSE®" and "Russell®" are trade marks of the relevant LSE Group companies and is/are used by any other LSE Group company under license. Neither LSE Group nor its licensors accept any liability for any errors or omissions in the indexes or data and no party may rely on any indexes or data contained in this communication. No further distribution of data from the LSE Group is permitted without the relevant LSE Group company's express written consent. The LSE Group does not promote, sponsor or endorse the content of this communication.

The Russell 1000 Index tracks the performance of 1000 of the largest U.S. companies, based on market capitalization.

The Russell 1000 Value Index measures the performance of those Russell 1000 Index companies with lower price-to-book ratios and lower forecasted growth values.

The Russell 1000 Growth Index measures the performance of those Russell 1000 Index companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher forecasted growth values.

Information provided by third parties is deemed to be reliable but may be derived using methodologies or techniques that are proprietary or specific to the third party source.

Original Post
